Typical time: Oct–Nov (Kartik Shukla Chaturthi–Saptami; ~1 week after Diwali)
Core celebration (what people do): 4-day vrat (Nahay-Khay, Kharna, Sandhya Arghya, Usha Arghya); arghya to setting + rising Sun at rivers/ponds; offerings like thekua in bamboo soop/daura
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Sun-worship with arghya to BOTH setting and rising Sun + strict 36-hour nirjala fast is far more central and mass-participatory than most states
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar treats it as the biggest mass festival across cities/villages; East UP also celebrates, but Bihar has denser ghat culture and statewide ‘homecoming’ travel

Core celebration (what people do): Same 4-day structure as Kartik Chhath but in Chaitra season
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: A rare ‘second Chhath’ many states don’t observe at scale
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Observed in both Bihar and pockets of UP; Bihar has larger household continuity (mannat-based repeats)

Core celebration (what people do): Girls/women make clay figurines (birds, Sama, Chakeva), sing Maithili folk songs, carry idols in baskets; immersion/farewell rituals
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Distinctly Mithila—folk-art + migratory-bird symbolism and women-led public singing sets it apart from mainstream pan-India festivals
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Mostly Bihar (Mithila belt) vs UP: largely not a mainstream UP fair; closest UP parallel is folk fairs but not this sibling/bird-idol tradition

Core celebration (what people do): Newly married women observe 13–15 day rituals; folk tales, songs; worship involving Gauri and serpent/Naga traditions
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Strongly marriage-rite oriented and region-specific (Mithila), unlike broader Shravan observances elsewhere
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar (Mithila) has this bridal focus; UP Shravan observances are more temple/kanwar oriented than a bride-centered 15-day vrata

Core celebration (what people do): Community cooking, aripan courtyard art, arghya to the moon; inclusive participation
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Notable because it reverses the common ‘avoid moon’ idea linked with Ganesh Chaturthi in many places
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar (Mithila) actively worships the moon; UP typically follows wider North Indian Ganesh Chaturthi narratives without Chaurchan prominence

Core celebration (what people do): Mass fair at Ganga–Gandak confluence; holy dip + Harihar Nath temple visit; livestock trade and stalls
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: One of Asia’s biggest cattle fairs; strong blend of pilgrimage + large-scale rural trade
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar hosts the canonical event at Sonepur; UP has large melas too but not this specific confluence cattle-fair identity

Core celebration (what people do): Pilgrims perform pind-daan/shraddha at Vishnupad/ghats; many-day ritual packages
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Gaya’s pind-daan is uniquely iconic nationally; other states do shraddha at home/nearby tirthas, but Gaya is a primary destination
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar has Gaya as a dedicated shraddha hub; UP has Prayagraj/Varanasi rituals but Gaya-style pind-daan pilgrimage concentration is distinct

Festival/Fair: Shravani Mela / Kanwar Yatra route (Sultanganj origin)
Core celebration (what people do): Kanwariyas collect Ganga jal at Sultanganj and walk long route to offer at Baidyanath Dham (Deoghar)
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Scale + cross-state foot pilgrimage corridor is a defining feature vs many local Shravan temple visits elsewhere
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar’s key role is Sultanganj jal-collection gateway; UP has Kanwar routes too, but this Bihar-to-Deoghar corridor is a hallmark

Core celebration (what people do): Worship of Manasa/Bishahari; legend of Bihula; associated folk art traditions (Manjusha)
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: The linked folk narrative + local art form makes it more culturally ‘story+art’ than typical temple-only puja
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar (Bhagalpur belt) is core; UP has snake-worship elements in Nag Panchami, but not the Bihula-Manjusha cultural package

Core celebration (what people do): Multi-day cultural performances (folk/classical), crafts, tourism events
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: More of a state-showcase cultural festival than a strictly religious mela
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar’s Rajgir-centric cultural branding; UP has similar mahotsavs (Lucknow/Agra/Varanasi) but different themes/heritage anchors

Typical time: Occurs in Adhika Maas years (every ~2–3 years)
Core celebration (what people do): Month-long pilgrimage-style fair during extra lunar month; sermons/worship; big inflow
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Extra-month (Adhika Maas) mass fair is unusual and time-dependent; not a fixed annual festival
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: Bihar’s Rajgir focus; UP has major monthly tirth periods too, but Rajgir Malmas has its own localized identity

Core celebration (what people do): Pilgrims camp and bathe at Sangam; religious discourses and rituals
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Prayagraj’s Sangam bathing-camps are especially central vs smaller river dips elsewhere
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP’s Prayagraj Sangam is the centerpiece; Bihar has big river rituals (e.g., Chhath) but not an equivalent Magh-month Sangam camp tradition

Core celebration (what people do): Mass bathing, akharas/saints, ritual camps at Sangam
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Scale and akhara-centric procession culture is unusually large compared with ordinary fairs
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP’s Prayagraj Kumbh is a global-scale congregation; Bihar has massive pilgrimages (Gaya/Sonepur/Chhath) but not Kumbh-style akhara encampments

Core celebration (what people do): Open-air, town-as-stage enactment across locations; audience moves with scenes
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Long-duration, immersive “moving pilgrimage” style is distinctive vs shorter stage Ramlilas elsewhere
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP (Ramnagar) is uniquely long and site-specific; Bihar has Ramlilas too, but typically shorter/local-stage formats

Core celebration (what people do): Large-scale diya-lighting along Saryu ghats; Ramayana-themed cultural performances; record attempts
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Ayodhya’s record-setting mass diya installations are a standout modern scale compared with most cities
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP’s Ayodhya emphasizes Ram-temple-centered spectacle; Bihar’s Diwali is widespread but not this single-city record-centric format

Core celebration (what people do): Ghats lit with vast diyas, Ganga aarti, lamps on river; major crowd management at ghats
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: More ghat-centric, riverfront illumination than typical Diwali lighting in other places; tied to Kartik Purnima
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP’s Varanasi ghat illumination is signature; Bihar has riverfront festivals (Chhath) but Dev-Deepawali style focuses on ghats + Kartik Purnima lamps rather than Sun arghya

Core celebration (what people do): Playful ritualized ‘lathi’ reenactment linked to Radha-Krishna lore; temple-centered celebrations
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Much more region-specific performance tradition than standard color-throwing Holi
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP’s Braj Holi is highly localized and lore-driven; Bihar Holi is vibrant too but typically lacks the Barsana-style lathmar enactment

Core celebration (what people do): Month-long fair with worship and commerce at Nauchandi Ground; associated with both temple and nearby dargah traditions
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: Known for syncretic Hindu–Muslim confluence alongside large bazaar/entertainment, which is not universal in other states’ fairs
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP’s Meerut mela has a strong documented syncretic identity; Bihar has syncretic fairs too, but Nauchandi’s specific pairing and scale are UP-local

Core celebration (what people do): Crafts bazaar + culture/cuisine showcases; performances by artists; artisan promotion
How it’s celebrated differently vs other: More “heritage + handicraft showcase” than a religious mela, unlike many Indian fairs
How it differs between Bihar and Uttar P: UP’s Agra version highlights Braj/UP craftsmanship; Bihar has craft + culture festivals too, but Taj Mahotsav is tightly tied to Agra tourism branding
